description: Canonical source-of-truth for Bitcoin-backed lending provider terms. Referenced by all Bitcoin Capital deep dives. Update here, all pages reflect new data. Every claim has a lastVerified date and a verificationSource URL.

providers.name: Loan My Coins
providers.founderBackground: Andy and Peter (The Bitcoin Adviser, since 2016)
providers.notes: BTC-for-BTC loan. Pledge BTC, receive BTC (95% LTV), 5% fixed upfront fee, no compounding interest. No margin calls — BTC-denominated structure means LTV is constant. Multisig collaborative custody via TBA. LMC is an introducer; actual loan provider independent. Affiliated with BSA via Dalia's adviser relationship at TBA.

positioning.audienceDefinition: A holder who has maxed out accumulation via traditional savings, salary, and direct purchase. At that stage, further accumulation requires leveraging existing BTC rather than acquiring more from outside capital.
positioning.coreStrategy: Outperform BTC by using LMC to convert pledged BTC into liquidity (without triggering LTCG), wait for a meaningful BTC drawdown within the 12mo–5yr term, buy back BTC at the lower price, deliver original BTC quantity to LMC at term end, and pocket the surplus.

strategyMechanics.step1: Pledge X BTC to LMC. Receive 0.95X BTC. No taxable event — pledging is not selling.
strategyMechanics.step2: Sell the 0.95X BTC for fiat. Park in T-bills or short-term yield.
strategyMechanics.step3: Wait for a meaningful BTC drawdown within the loan term (high probability over 5 years given historical volatility).
strategyMechanics.step4: Buy back BTC at the lower price. A $950K cash position from a 10 BTC pledge buys 19 BTC if BTC halves.
strategyMechanics.step5: Deliver X BTC to LMC at term end. Original X BTC collateral returns.
strategyMechanics.step6: Net result: more BTC than the holder started with, accomplished without triggering capital gains.

exampleMath.deliverLMC: Deliver 10 BTC to LMC for repayment
exampleMath.finalPosition: 10 BTC + $450K cash (equivalent to 19 BTC at $50K BTC price)

whenItWorks: BTC has a meaningful drawdown within the loan term
Borrower has discipline to deploy cash at the dip, not panic-sell or chase rallies
Borrower's cost basis is low enough that LTCG deferral has material value
Long-term BTC conviction — this is accumulation, not exit
whenItFails: BTC rallies steadily without significant drawdowns (5% upfront fee + lost upside on pledged BTC during the loan term)
Borrower can't time the buyback effectively (cash sits in T-bills earning 4–5% while BTC compounds higher)
BTC has risen meaningfully at term end and borrower can't roll OR repay — collateral forfeit, taxable event at FMV
Borrower has high cost basis — tax deferral benefit is small
positioning.honestFraming: This is not a hedge. It is a directional bet on BTC volatility with a 5-year window. Works for terminal holders who already maxed accumulation and want exposure to BTC's drawdown cycle as an accumulation mechanism. Wrong for new entrants, those in the wealth-building phase, or anyone trying to hedge a separate mortgage's margin-call risk.

wrongFitWhen: Needs fiat (must sell received BTC, friction)
30-year horizon (compounding 5% rolls add up)
Wants margin call protection through hedge (it's not a hedge)
High cost basis (small tax-deferral benefit)

keyDistribution.borrower: 1 of 3 keys, held personally (hardware wallet, seed-phrase backup)
keyDistribution.lender: 1 of 3 keys, held by TBA on behalf of LMC
keyDistribution.keyAgent: 1 of 3 keys, held by independent regulated trust or qualified third party

failureModes.lenderInsolvency: Borrower + Key Agent recover via 2-of-3. Bankruptcy estate has no claim on multisig.
failureModes.borrowerKeyLoss: TBA + Key Agent co-sign with identity verification to recover.
failureModes.twoOfThreeCollusion: Theoretically possible; mitigated by separate legal entities with fiduciary duties + on-chain visibility making any movement immediately detectable.
failureModes.borrowerDefault: TBA + Key Agent co-sign liquidation per loan agreement (contractual, not unilateral).

custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: strong — LMC/TBA never had sole control, so BTC is never on their balance sheet
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: TBA collaborative custody architecture in production since 2016 with no reported losses

providers.name: Ledn

terms.interestType: monthly interest, no required monthly payments

providers.founderBackground: Adam Reeds, Mauricio Di Bartolomeo (Canadian-founded, BTC-focused since 2018, survived 2022 cohort collapse)
providers.notes: BTC-for-fiat loan. Borrower chooses Standard (rehypothecation allowed, 12.4% APR) or Custodied (ring-fenced, 13.9% APR) at origination. Segregated on-chain addresses, legally ring-fenced from funding partner bankruptcy. Tiered rates scale down with loan size. No credit check, no required monthly payments.
bestFitWhen: Need USD or USDC liquidity
Want regulated, mainstream BTC lender
Comfortable with margin call mechanics
Loan size $1M+ for best rate tier
wrongFitWhen: Want zero margin-call exposure
Prefer self-custody or multisig
Sovereignty-first borrower uncomfortable with rehypothecation (use Custodied option but accept higher rate)

custodyAnalysis.custodian: BitGo (regulated trust company, $250M insurance, audited cold storage)

keyDistribution.lender: Ledn directs operations but does not hold custody keys
keyDistribution.custodian: BitGo holds keys via internal multisig (3-of-N internal, not split with borrower)

custodied: Ledn's funding partner failure (mitigated by ring-fenced segregated addresses)
BitGo failure (mitigated by insurance + regulatory protection)
standard: PLUS counterparty institutions that BTC is rehypothecated to

failureModes.lenderInsolvency_custodied: Ring-fenced segregated addresses; collateral should be returnable via bankruptcy-remote structure
failureModes.lenderInsolvency_standard: Rehypothecated collateral may be trapped if counterparty also affected; recovery depends on counterparty solvency chain
failureModes.custodianInsolvency: BitGo insurance ($250M) + regulatory protection; collateral recovery via insolvency proceeding
failureModes.borrowerLosesAccountAccess: KYC/identity recovery; collateral remains accessible after re-verification
failureModes.rehypothecationCounterpartyFails_standar: Loss event for Ledn; could pass through to borrowers if Ledn lacks capital reserves to cover

rehypothecation.tradeoff: 1.5% APR premium for non-rehypothecation (13.9% Custodied vs 12.4% Standard)

bankruptcyRemoteness.custodied: strong in theory — ring-fenced segregated addresses with legal opinion
bankruptcyRemoteness.standard: weaker — rehypothecated portion is off the protected addresses, recovery depends on counterparty solvency

custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Survived the 2022 cohort collapse (Celsius, BlockFi, Voyager, Genesis) due to: term-matched book, limited rehypothecation, counterparty diversification, and the qualified-custodian structure

providers.name: Unchained Capital

structure.productPositioning: Commercial loans (B2B) — pivoted from personal loans

providers.founderBackground: Joe Kelly, Dhruv Bansal (Bitcoin-focused since 2016, pioneers of collaborative custody)
providers.trackRecord: $1B+ in loan originations, 1,000+ loans originated, zero lost bitcoin (per company claim)
providers.verifyNote: Unchained pivoted to commercial (B2B) loans. Current public terms: 14% interest rate, 16.21% APR (origination included), $150K minimum, 12-payment terms. NMLS ID: 1900773. Previously offered tiered personal loan rates 9.99%-11.49%; that product appears to be discontinued in favor of the commercial loans product.
providers.notes: BTC-for-fiat commercial loan with collaborative 2-of-3 multisig custody. Borrower holds 1 key, Unchained 1, Fortis Bank 1 (key partner shift from prior independent key agent). Collateral in segregated multisig address, fully verifiable on-chain. No rehypothecation. Interest-only monthly payments over 12-payment term. $150K minimum. Now positioned as business/commercial product, not retail/personal.
bestFitWhen: Need USD with sovereignty-leaning custody
Loan size $150K+
Want 2-of-3 multisig with own key custody
Already familiar with Unchained's collaborative-custody architecture (existing vault customers)
wrongFitWhen: Loan under $150K (minimum is binding)
Want fully passive (interest-only payments still required monthly)
International (US-only)

keyDistribution.keyAgent: 1 of 3 keys, held by independent key agent

proofOfFunds.method: on-chain verifiable via Unchained dashboard + independent block explorer

failureModes.lenderInsolvency: Borrower + Key Agent recover via 2-of-3. Same architecture as LMC.
failureModes.borrowerKeyLoss: Unchained + Key Agent co-sign with identity verification to recover.
failureModes.twoOfThreeCollusion: Same risk profile as LMC; mitigated by separate legal entities + on-chain visibility.
failureModes.borrowerDefault: Unchained + Key Agent co-sign liquidation per loan agreement.

custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Unchained pioneered collaborative custody for retail Bitcoin in 2017. Multisig vault architecture battle-tested across the BTC space for nearly a decade.
custodyAnalysis.structuralDifferencesFromLMC: Operational differences only: longer track record, different key-agent counterparty, larger institutional client base, different fee/rate model

providers.name: Lava

apr.stepUp: rate increases monthly to incentivize quick repayment

terms.interestType: step-up APR, no required monthly payments
terms.technology: Discreet Log Contracts (DLCs) — on-chain settlement, no custodian

providers.verifyTodo: LTV figure (60%) from third-party source — confirm directly with Lava
providers.notes: BTC-for-fiat loan via Discreet Log Contracts (DLCs). Fully self-custodial — borrower retains full key control of BTC via on-chain contract. Step-up APR (5% → 11.5% over 12 months) incentivizes quick repayment. Visa card available with up to 5% BTC cashback. Newest entrant; less track record than Ledn/Unchained but most sovereignty-aligned custody model.
bestFitWhen: Want full self-custody of BTC even during loan term
Comfortable with DLC technology and step-up APR
Need fast processing or small loan amounts ($100+)
Plan to repay quickly to lock in low early-month rates
wrongFitWhen: Want long-term low-rate borrowing (rate climbs to 11.5% by month 12)
Uncomfortable with DLC technology / newer protocol
Want established track record (Lava is newer than Ledn/Unchained)

custodyAnalysis.technology: Discreet Log Contracts (DLCs) — on-chain Bitcoin smart contract construct using oracle attestations

keyDistribution.borrower: sole custody — borrower funds the DLC and holds all relevant keys
keyDistribution.lender: no keys over the BTC — Lava is counterparty to the DLC, not custodian
keyDistribution.oracle: price oracle attests to BTC price for triggering DLC settlement events (e.g., liquidation thresholds)

custodyAnalysis.signatureThreshold: borrower-only for movement outside DLC terms; DLC executes per encoded logic

proofOfFunds.note: strongest proof-of-funds model in the matrix — Lava cannot lie about reserves because they don't hold them

spofsForCollateral: borrower's own private key — no recovery if lost
spofsForProtocol: DLC implementation bugs (newer technology, less battle-tested than multisig)
Lava insolvency mid-loan (DLC continues but loan servicing may be impaired)

failureModes.lavaInsolvency: Borrower retains full custody. DLC runs independently. Loan obligation may become unenforceable or transferred to a receiver. Risk: USD liquidity Lava was supposed to provide stops, but pledged BTC is safe.
failureModes.borrowerKeyLoss: BTC is permanently lost. No recovery mechanism — Lava has no keys to help. This is the trade-off for full self-custody.
failureModes.oracleAttack: DLC settlement may be disrupted or manipulated. Lava has implemented oracle redundancy but this is a category of risk that doesn't exist in multisig.
failureModes.priceBasedLiquidation: DLC executes per contract: Lava receives the BTC, borrower keeps the USD already disbursed. Automatic, on-chain, no cure window.
failureModes.borrowerDefault: Same as price-based liquidation — DLC settlement executes per contract.

custodyAnalysis.rehypothecation: impossible by design — Lava never has the keys
custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: strongest of all models — Lava's bankruptcy is structurally irrelevant to the BTC; the legal claim is encoded in the DLC, not on Lava's balance sheet
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Newest of the four; raised $17.5M; growing user base; less stress-tested than multisig models
custodyAnalysis.tradeoffSummary: Maximum sovereignty (no third-party signature at all), but borrower bears full key-loss risk and protocol-edge-case risk

tier2Providers.name: Coinbase (via Morpho)

terms.apr: variable, set by Morpho protocol supply/demand, updates per-block on Base
terms.maximumUnit: USDC against BTC ($1M against ETH; multi-asset support)
terms.wrappedAsset: cbBTC (Coinbase Wrapped Bitcoin) — ERC-20 token 1:1 backed by BTC in Coinbase custody

tier2Providers.notes: BTC-backed loan via Coinbase Wrapped BTC (cbBTC) locked in Morpho smart contract on Base blockchain. $2.17B originated as of April 14, 2026. Structurally a hybrid — centralized custody (Coinbase holds actual BTC) + DeFi mechanics (Morpho handles lending logic). Not a pure BTC-only product since cbBTC is an ERC-20 wrapper, but Coinbase's institutional credibility and Morpho's protocol maturity make it a meaningful market presence.
bestFitWhen: Already a Coinbase customer with BTC on the platform
Want regulated-exchange + DeFi-mechanics combination
Loan size $5M+ ceiling needed
Comfortable with wrapped BTC (cbBTC) and Base blockchain
wrongFitWhen: Want BTC-native lending (avoid wrapped tokens)
Uncomfortable with smart contract risk (Morpho protocol)
Self-custody priority (your BTC becomes cbBTC then locked in smart contract)
Want fixed-rate predictability (rates change per block)

keyDistribution.borrower: no keys over the underlying BTC; controls Coinbase account
keyDistribution.custodian: Coinbase holds the actual BTC backing the cbBTC tokens
keyDistribution.smartContract: Morpho smart contract on Base holds the cbBTC during the loan

custodyAnalysis.signatureThreshold: Coinbase account access (centralized) + smart contract execution (deterministic)

proofOfFunds.borrowerCanVerify: indirectly — borrower verifies cbBTC, must trust Coinbase's 1:1 backing claim
proofOfFunds.auditCycleDependency: yes for BTC backing, no for cbBTC supply

spofsForCollateral: cbBTC peg failure (theoretical depeg from underlying BTC)

failureModes.coinbaseInsolvency: Underlying BTC backing cbBTC at risk; cbBTC could depeg or become unredeemable. Major risk vector.
failureModes.morphoSmartContractBug: Funds locked in contract could be exposed to exploit; Morpho is audited but smart contract risk is real
failureModes.baseChainHalt: Loan operations may pause; collateral safe but liquidations may not trigger normally
failureModes.cbBTCPegFailure: If cbBTC trades below 1:1 with BTC, borrowers face complex unwinding
failureModes.borrowerDefault: Smart contract auto-liquidates at 86% LTV with 4.38% penalty

custodyAnalysis.rehypothecation: false at the loan level (cbBTC locked in smart contract); unclear what Coinbase does with underlying BTC reserves
custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: weaker — Coinbase holds the underlying BTC. In Coinbase bankruptcy, the cbBTC backing is a legal claim, not a direct asset.
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Re-entrant product (Coinbase exited lending in 2022 with Coinbase Borrow shutdown); current Morpho integration grew rapidly

tier2Providers.name: SALT Lending

structure.rehypothecationDisclosure: Explicitly permitted in loan agreement: SALT may 'repledge, sell or otherwise transfer or use Stored Coins'

terms.supportedCollateral: Multi-asset (BTC primary, also ETH and others)

tier2Providers.founderBackground: Founded 2016 — original BTC-backed lender, predates Ledn
tier2Providers.notes: Original BTC-backed lender (2016). Multi-asset (not BTC-only), which is why we tier-2 them rather than tier-1. Rehypothecation explicitly allowed in terms — borrowers should understand this is structurally similar to the 2022 cohort's risk profile, although SALT survived 2022. Custody via BitGo and/or Fireblocks (qualified custodians). Borrower-selectable LTV (30/50/70%) is a useful flexibility feature.
bestFitWhen: Want flexible LTV selection (30/50/70%)
Comfortable with rehypothecation in exchange for competitive rates
Need multi-year term (1, 3, or 5 year options)
Already familiar with SALT from pre-2022 era
wrongFitWhen: Sovereignty-first priority — rehypothecation is a non-starter
Concerned about 2022-style cohort risks
Want BTC-only lender (SALT is multi-asset)
Prefer modern multisig custody

keyDistribution.lender: SALT directs operations, does not hold keys directly
keyDistribution.custodian: BitGo/Fireblocks holds keys via internal multisig

proofOfFunds.method: custodian SOC 2 Type 2 audits; no independent audit of SALT's application layer per Zone21
proofOfFunds.audit: Custodians audited (SOC 2 Type 2); SALT itself has periodic pen-tests, no independent app-layer audit per third-party reviewers

spofsForCollateral: SALT failure (mitigated only partially — rehypothecated collateral has exposure to counterparty institutions)

failureModes.saltInsolvency: Rehypothecated collateral may be trapped in bankruptcy if counterparty institutions are also affected. Higher recovery risk than non-rehypothecating peers.
failureModes.custodianInsolvency: BitGo/Fireblocks have insurance and regulatory protection; recovery via insolvency proceeding
failureModes.rehypothecationCounterpartyFails: Loss event; recovery depends on SALT's capital adequacy and the failed institution's bankruptcy estate

custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: weak — rehypothecation moves collateral off any segregated structure; recovery in bankruptcy depends on counterparty solvency chain
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Survived 2022 cohort failures despite rehypothecation model; longest track record (since 2016) but less sovereignty-aligned than 2022-onward entrants

tier2Providers.name: Arch Lending

terms.supportedCollateral: BTC (60% LTV), ETH (55% LTV), SOL (45% LTV)

tier2Providers.notes: Multi-asset lender (BTC, ETH, SOL) with strict no-rehypothecation policy. Custody via Anchorage Digital — a federally chartered bank — with segregated wallets and $100M Lloyd's of London insurance. Effective APR on BTC program ~14% when combining 12.5% interest + 1.5% origination, despite 7.25% headline rate (which is for largest tiers). Cleanest Tier 2 sovereignty option besides Debifi.
bestFitWhen: Want regulated qualified-custodian model without rehypothecation
Need 24-month term (longer than Bitfinex, shorter than SALT's 5yr)
BTC loan size where 60% LTV makes sense
Want refinance optionality
wrongFitWhen: Want multi-year term beyond 24 months
Want BTC-only lender (Arch is multi-asset)
Need very low APR (effective rate ~14% on BTC program)
Want multisig collaborative custody

custodyAnalysis.custodian: Anchorage Digital — federally chartered bank, $100M Lloyd's of London insurance

keyDistribution.custodian: Anchorage holds keys via internal multisig in segregated wallets

proofOfFunds.method: Anchorage's federal-chartered-bank status + Lloyd's insurance attestations

spofsForCollateral: Arch failure (mitigated by segregated wallets at Anchorage)
Anchorage failure (federally chartered bank with insurance — lower risk than typical custodian)

failureModes.archInsolvency: Collateral in segregated wallets at Anchorage; bankruptcy-remote in principle
failureModes.anchorageInsolvency: Federal banking regulatory protection + $100M insurance; one of the lowest-risk custodians available
failureModes.borrowerDefault: Arch uses tiered safeguards: starting LTV conservative, early notifications, margin calls, partial liquidations to restore loan health

custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: strong — segregated wallets at federally chartered bank
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Newer entrant (post-2022 era); Anchorage Digital custody is one of the strongest qualified-custodian options available

tier2Providers.name: Bitfinex Borrow

terms.apr: variable: USD lending 5–15% APR typically, spiking 30%+ in volatility
terms.marketStructure: P2P matching of borrowers and lenders on Bitfinex platform

tier2Providers.notes: Fundamentally different from the other Tier 1/2 lenders. This is exchange margin lending, not a traditional loan product. Loans are 2-120 days, P2P matched, with margin call mechanics built into the exchange. Notable: 2016 Bitfinex hack lost 119,756 BTC — exchange custody risk is real and historically demonstrated. Tether/USDT parent association brings regulatory uncertainty. Useful for short-term traders, not for hodlers seeking multi-year liquidity.
bestFitWhen: Need short-term USD liquidity (days to weeks)
Already active on Bitfinex with BTC on the platform
Comfortable with exchange custody risk
Can manage frequent rollover (2–120 day terms)
wrongFitWhen: Need multi-year liquidity (terms are too short)
Hodler-style sovereignty priority (exchange custody is the antithesis)
US-based and risk-averse on regulatory exposure
Want predictable fixed-term loan product

custodyAnalysis.custodian: Bitfinex (centralized exchange) — borrower's BTC sits in exchange wallet/margin system

keyDistribution.exchange: Bitfinex holds all keys via internal custody system

spofsForCollateral: Bitfinex itself (the entire exchange = single point of failure for all customer funds)

failureModes.bitfinexInsolvency: All customer funds at risk; recovery via insolvency proceeding (questionable given offshore jurisdiction)
failureModes.bitfinexHack: Historical precedent (2016) — customer losses were partially repaid via BFX tokens, but recovery was lengthy and contentious
failureModes.regulatoryAction: US regulatory pressure on Tether/Bitfinex affiliates could disrupt operations
failureModes.marginCallExecution: P2P automated margin enforcement; borrower has limited recourse

custodyAnalysis.rehypothecation: exchange-pooled — Bitfinex effectively has full control of collateral
custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: weak — offshore exchange, customer funds historically commingled
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: 2016 hack is the major event; survived with controversial repayment via BFX tokens; long operational history but high regulatory uncertainty
custodyAnalysis.structuralCaveat: This is exchange margin lending, NOT a dedicated lending product. Categorically different risk profile from the other providers in this deep dive.

tier2Providers.name: Debifi
tier2Providers.notes: Non-custodial P2P bitcoin-backed lending with 3-of-4 multisig. Borrower, lender, Debifi, and AnchorWatch (independent key holder) each hold one key. Institutional-focused. Notable 2026 development: Sygnum Bank partnership (MultiSYG) launching H1 2026 — first regulated-bank-backed multisig BTC lending. Newer entrant; institutional focus means less retail availability but strong sovereignty model.
bestFitWhen: Institutional borrower (HNW, family office, business)
Want non-custodial multisig with borrower key
European or Swiss jurisdiction preference
Looking for regulated-bank-backed option (via Sygnum partnership)
wrongFitWhen: Retail borrower (institutional-focused)
Need US-domiciled lender
Want established multi-year operational track record
Need fast onboarding (institutional KYC takes longer)

keyDistribution.lender: 1 of 4 keys (the actual liquidity provider, varies per loan)

failureModes.debifiInsolvency: Borrower + lender + AnchorWatch can recover via 3 of 4 keys without Debifi
failureModes.borrowerKeyLoss: Lender + Debifi + AnchorWatch can recover via 3 of 4 with verification
failureModes.borrowerDefault: Lender + Debifi + AnchorWatch sign liquidation per loan terms

custodyAnalysis.bankruptcyRemoteness: strong — multisig with no single-party control
custodyAnalysis.trackRecord: Newer than LMC/Unchained; Sygnum partnership (H1 2026) brings regulated-bank credibility; structurally most redundant multisig in the market (3-of-4 vs. 2-of-3)
custodyAnalysis.structuralAdvantageOverLMCUnchained: 3-of-4 vs 2-of-3 gives more redundancy — any one party can fail without blocking the others; trade-off is more coordination complexity for routine operations

excluded.note: Mentioned with reason in deep dive; not covered in depth.

providers.reason: Multi-asset crypto lender — dilutes Bitcoin focus
DeFi / on-chain — separate deep dive eventually
Primarily payments; credit experiments not core product
Multi-asset; BTC is included but not BTC-only positioning
